content.full_text:
July 8, 1992


Jeanne E. Parrish
Councilwoman, District #2
City of Rancho Mirage
69-825 Highway 111
Rancho Mirage, CA 92270-2898

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. G-92-391

Dear Councilwoman Parrish:

This is in reply to your letter to our Chairman, Ben
Davidian, dated July 1, 1992. You letter seeks legal advice
concerning the open meeting laws.

This agency has no jurisdiction over the open meeting laws in
California and, therefore, we cannot provide answers to your
specific questions.

We understand that the State Attorney General's Office
provides advice on the open meeting laws. Should you require
immediate assistance you may want to telephone Deputy Attorney
General Ted Prim at (916) 324-5481.

I am sorry we could not be of help to you on this matter.

Sincerely,

[signature]

SCOTT HALLABRIN
Acting General Counsel


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.question_synthetic: Councilwoman Parrish sought legal advice from the FPPC concerning California open meeting laws and their application to her circumstances.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice because it lacks jurisdiction over open meeting laws in California. The FPPC referred Councilwoman Parrish to the State Attorney General's Office, specifically suggesting she contact Deputy Attorney General Ted Prim for assistance.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is a declination letter with no formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections. The document clearly indicates FPPC's lack of jurisdiction over the subject matter and provides a referral to the appropriate agency.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Councilwoman Parrish sought legal advice from the FPPC concerning California open meeting laws and their application to her circumstances.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declined to provide advice because it lacks jurisdiction over open meeting laws in California. The FPPC referred Councilwoman Parrish to the State Attorney General's Office, specifically suggesting she contact Deputy Attorney General Ted Prim for assistance.
